coredata.dc:title: Cytotoxicity and autophagy induction by graphene quantum dots with different functional groups
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Yichun
Wan, Bin
Yang, Yu
Cui, Xuejing
Xin, Yan
Guo, Liang-Hong
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess great potential in various applications due to their superior physicochemical properties and wide array of available surface modifications. However, the toxicity of GQDs has not been systematically assessed, thus hindered their further development; especially, the risk of surface modifications of GQDs is largely unknown. In this study, we employed a lung carcinoma A549 cells as the model to investigate the cytotoxicity and autophagy induction of three types GQDs, including cGQDs (COOH-GQDs), hGQDs (OH-GQDs), and aGQDs (NH2-GQDs). The results showed hGQDs was the most toxic, as significant cell death was induced at the concentration of 100 μg/mL, determining by WST-1 assay as well as Annexin-V-FITC/PI apoptosis analysis, whereas cGQDs and aGQDs were non-cytotoxic within the measured concentration. Autophagy detection was performed by TEM examination, LC3 fluorescence tracking, and Western-blot. Both aGQDs and hGQDs induced cellular autophagy to various degrees except for cGQDs. Further analysis on autophagy pathways indicated all GQDs significantly activated p-p38MAPK; p-ERK1/2 was inhibited by aGQDs and hGQDs but activated by cGQDs. p-JNK was inhibited by aGQDs and cGQDs, while activated by hGQDs. Simultaneously, Akt was activated by hGQDs but inhibited by aGQDs. Inhibition of autophagy by 3-MA significantly increased the cytotoxicity of GQDs, suggesting that autophagy played a protective role against the toxicity of GQDs. In conclusion, cGQDs showed excellent biocompatibility and may be considered for biological applications. Autophagy induction may be included in the health risk assessment of GQDs as it reflects the stress status which may eventually lead to diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cytotoxicity
Autophagy
Surface functional group

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TEM examination on ultrathin section of cells
Autophagosome accumulation in A549 cells upon GQDs exposure
Differential pathway activation by aGQDs, cGQDs and hGQDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of aGQDs, cGQDs, and hGQDs. (a) TEM images of three types of GQDs, scale bar = 10 nm; (b) Size distribution of three types of GQDs with the percentage specified above each column, as determined by analyzing GQDs dots on TEM images. Over 30 GQDs were measured for each GQDs; (c) FT-IR spectrum of three types of GQDs. The presence of C-N bond (υ = 1137 and 1190 cm−1) and N-H bond (υ = 866 and 3305 cm−1) on the surface of aGQDs, carboxyl groups (υ = 1635 cm−1, -COOH) on cGQDs and hydroxyl group (υ = 1397 cm−1, -OH) on hGQDs were detected.

Cytotoxicity of GQDs. (a) Cell viability change under the exposure of GQDs for 24 hr, as determined by WST-1 essay, and H2O2 treatment (1000 μmol/L, 2 hr) was set as positive control (POS); (b) Apoptosis detection of cells under the exposure of GQDs for 24 hr, as determined by flow cytometry using annexin-V-FITC/PI staining (T/C: Treatment/Control). (c) Quadrant analysis on flow data. Strauosporine (STO, 2 μmol/L, 4 hr) treatment was set as positive control. Asterisk indicates significant difference from control at level, ** p < 0.01. Data are presented as the means of three independent experiments. Error bars represent S.D.

Autophagosome accumulation in A549 cells under the exposure of 100 μg/mL of (a) aGQDs, (b) cGQDs and (c) hGQDs and 200 μg/mL of (d) aGQDs, (e) cGQDs and (f) hGQDs. AVd: degrading autophagic vacuoles; AVi: initial autophagic vacuoles. Yellow arrows direct to autophagic bodies; Star (⁎) indicates putative phagophore.

Autophagosome accumulation in A549 cells under the exposure of GQDs. (a) Representative laser confocal images of the formation of RFP-LC3 dots (Red) in Lv-A549 (stably transfected with RFP-LC3) and then exposed to medium containing GQDs at concentration of 100 and 200 μg/mL for 24 hr. (b) The corresponding average number of punctuates per cell was expressed as fold change over control by counting at least 30 cells. (c) Typical fluorescence images of A549 cells exposed to 100 μg/mL GQDs for 24 hr and stained by CYTO-ID® green, a specific autophagosome labeling probe for LC3 positive vesicles. Nuclei were stained with Hoechst 33,342. The corresponding relative green fluorescence intensity (RFI) per cell normalized against control (d). 10 μmol/L Chloroquine (CQ) was used as positive control, **p < 0.01. Scale bar = 10 μm.

Western-blot analysis on the conversion of LC3-I to LC3-II, the abundance of autophagic target p62 protein and Beclin-1 expression in A549 cells. Total protein was harvested and subjected to western-blot analysis with anti-LC3, anti-p62, or anti-beclin-1 antibody. β-actin served as loading control. The level of protein expression was measured by integrated optical densitometry (IOD) using Gelpro4.0 software. (a) Normalized LC3-II/LC3-I ratio against control, Chloroquine (CQ, 10 μmol/L) was used as a positive control. (b) Normalized expression of p62, Rapamycin (Rapa, 100 nmol/L) was used as a positive control. (c) Expression of Beclin-1. Error bars represent S.D. of at least three individual experiments. Asterisk indicates significant difference from control at level *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

MAPK and Akt signaling pathway affected by GQDs. (a) Fold changes and western blot images of p-p38/p38; p-ERK/ERK (b); p-JNK/JNK (c); and p-Akt/Akt (d) in A549 cells under the exposure of three types of GQDs. The level of protein expression was measured by integrated optical densitometry (IOD) using Gelpro4.0 software. GAPDH was used as loading control for MAPK pathway and β-actin for Akt pathway. Error bars represent S.D. of at least three individual experiments. Asterisk indicates significant difference from control at level *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

Effects of autophagy inhibition through 3-MA pretreatment on cell viability. (a) LC3-II/LC3-I ratio change in A549 cells exposed to 200 μg/mL aGQDs in the presence and absence of 3-MA pre-treatment. Lower panel: Western-blot image of LC3 (II/I) and β-actin, *p < .05; (b) Viability of A549 cells exposed to aGQDs and hGQDs in the presence and absence of 3-MA pre-treatment, as determined by WST-1 assay, **p < .01.

head.ce:title: Cytotoxicity and autophagy induction by graphene quantum dots with different functional groups

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry and Ecotoxicology, Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100085, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry and Ecotoxicology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory for Biomedical Effects of Nanomaterials and Nanosafety, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology of China, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory for Biomedical Effects of Nanomaterials and Nanosafety
National Center for Nanoscience and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environment and Health, Jianghan University, Wuhan 430056, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess great potential in various applications due to their superior physicochemical properties and wide array of available surface modifications. However, the toxicity of GQDs has not been systematically assessed, thus hindered their further development; especially, the risk of surface modifications of GQDs is largely unknown. In this study, we employed a lung carcinoma A549 cells as the model to investigate the cytotoxicity and autophagy induction of three types GQDs, including cGQDs (COOH-GQDs), hGQDs (OH-GQDs), and aGQDs (NH2-GQDs). The results showed hGQDs was the most toxic, as significant cell death was induced at the concentration of 100 μg/mL, determining by WST-1 assay as well as Annexin-V-FITC/PI apoptosis analysis, whereas cGQDs and aGQDs were non-cytotoxic within the measured concentration. Autophagy detection was performed by TEM examination, LC3 fluorescence tracking, and Western-blot. Both aGQDs and hGQDs induced cellular autophagy to various degrees except for cGQDs. Further analysis on autophagy pathways indicated all GQDs significantly activated p-p38MAPK; p-ERK1/2 was inhibited by aGQDs and hGQDs but activated by cGQDs. p-JNK was inhibited by aGQDs and cGQDs, while activated by hGQDs. Simultaneously, Akt was activated by hGQDs but inhibited by aGQDs. Inhibition of autophagy by 3-MA significantly increased the cytotoxicity of GQDs, suggesting that autophagy played a protective role against the toxicity of GQDs. In conclusion, cGQDs showed excellent biocompatibility and may be considered for biological applications. Autophagy induction may be included in the health risk assessment of GQDs as it reflects the stress status which may eventually lead to diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are small graphene fragments with typical size below 20 nm (Bacon et al., 2014). GQDs display favorable physicochemical properties that attract a great deal of attention in the field of optical, electrochemistry and biomedical applications. Compared with quantum dots (QDs), GQDs possess superior properties such as quantum confinement and edge effects (Bacon et al., 2014) but relatively lower toxicity due to the lack of heavy metal components and therefore are considered as a promising alternative in biomedical application (Chong et al., 2014). In addition, one of the most desirable properties of GQDs is its massive capabilities of surface functionalization (Bacon et al., 2014), which can tune the band-gap of GQDs. Ideally, regulation of surface chemistry can manipulate and diversify the property of GQDs (Zhu et al., 2012). In fact, functionalization was shown to alter photoluminescence (Sun et al., 2013), biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2013), and electronic properties (Feng et al., 2013) as well as to enhance optical properties and quantum yields of GQDs (Bacon et al., 2014). With such excellent flexibility of property modification, it is expected that applications of GQDs in the field of bio-imaging, biosensor, and drug delivery will become a promising area (Bacon et al., 2014). With the increasing interest in GQDs applications in biomedical fields, concerns are raised on their potential toxicity and influence on human health.

The cytotoxicity of GQDs has been examined in several cancer cell lines including gastric and breast cancer cells (Wu et al., 2013), as well as lung cancer cells (Yuan et al., 2014). The results suggested a generally low toxicity of GQDs to these cells. In vivo, the toxicity and distribution of carboxylated GQDs have been examined and the results showed no toxicity in mice (Nurunnabi et al., 2013). However, previous studies showed that surface functionalization of nanoparticles might simultaneously induce unexpected effects on cells such as autophagy (Li et al., 2015b) and apoptosis (Akiyoshi et al., 2004; Hexel, 2009; Qiu et al., 2010; Shubenkov et al., 2014; Wan et al., 2015; Yin et al., 2010; Yuan et al., 2014). For nanoparticles, surface chemistry is a dictating factor which influences their toxicity (Akiyoshi et al., 2004; Yin et al., 2010). For example, surface coating of ZnO (zinc oxide) nanoparticles with PMAA (poly-methacrylic acid) can abate the intracellular ROS generation and the toxicity in WIL2-NS human lymphoblastoid cells (Yin et al., 2010). In contrast, amine-functionalization on the surface of CdSe/ZnS QDs induces higher toxicity than carboxylated one does and the cytotoxicity of these QDs depends on their surface molecules rather than the nanocrystal itself (Akiyoshi et al., 2004). Therefore, the question rises as how surface modifications will influence the cytotoxicity of GQDs. Although Yuan et al. found low cytotoxicity of GQDs with several different functional groups including –NH2, –COOH, and –CO-N(CH3)2; the surface modifications did not influence the cytotoxicity neither (Yuan et al., 2014). But the study only focused on the cell viability of GQDs, while ignoring their effects on autophagy.

In general, the occurrence and mode of cell death are dependent on the cell type, energy metabolism level, and environmental stimulus (Lockshin and Zakeri, 2004). Besides apoptosis and necrosis, a lysosome-based degradation process named cell autophagy was recently proposed as an important pathway of cell death (Stern et al., 2012). Autophagy involves cell degradation of unnecessary or dysfunctional cellular components through the lysosomal machinery. During this process, targeted cytoplasmic constituents are isolated from the rest of the cell within the double-membraned autophagosome, which are then fused with lysosomes and degraded or recycled (Yoshimori, 2004). Cellular autophagic activity is usually under basal condition, but can be markedly upregulated by numerous stimuli such as nutrient starvation, innate immune signals, chemical reagents, and infections (Levine and Kroemer, 2008). Autophagy has been seen as an adaptive response to survival, whereas unconstrained autophagy appears to promote cell death and morbidity (Mizushima et al., 2010; Patel et al., 2012). Dysfunction of autophagy contributes to many diseases such as cancer (Amaravadi et al., 2016) and neurodegenerative diseases (Kiriyama and Nochi, 2015). In addition, the breakdown of the lysosomal degradation system may induce a cellular traffic jam which in turn leads to pathological outcomes, such as heart failure (Knaapen et al., 2001), Alzheimer's disease (Boland et al., 2008), Huntington disease (Heng et al., 2010), and Fabry disease (Chevrier et al., 2010).

Extensive studies suggested that autophagy could be modulated by surface chemistry of nanoparticles (Chang et al., 2015; Li et al., 2015b; Mondal et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2012). For example, Wu et al. (2014) compared the autophagy induction ability of 81 different surface-modified multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and found autophagy induction ability varies significantly among different surface functionalized MWCNTs. Seven MWCNTs including MWCNT-COOH display the strongest autophagy induction ability, and 41 of them exhibit medium autophagy induction ability, whereas 26 modifications have little influence on the autophagy levels. Also, it has been reported that GQDs with or without irradiation of blue light could both induce autophagy through generation of ROS (Markovic et al., 2012; Qin et al., 2015) and p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) and nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB)-mediated signaling pathways (Qin et al., 2015). However, little is known about the influence of surface modification of GQDs on cell autophagy, which may contribute to or counteract cell death. Thus, it is of imminent significance to evaluate autophagy level induced by different functionalized GQDs. The results will promote a better understanding on the potential risks in applications of GQDs with different surface functional groups.

In this study, we employed a lung cancer cell line, A549, as the model to compare the potencies of three GQDs with different surface modifications including amination (aGQDs), carboxylation (cGQDs), and hydroxylation (hGQDs) in inducing cyto-toxicity and autophagy. Our results showed that different surface modifications of GQDs caused distinct effects on apoptosis and autophagy. cGQDs exhibit the best biocompatibility, with no significant influence on cell apoptosis and autophagy, whereas hGQDs caused a significant loss of cell viability while induced a rather low autophagy level. Although no significant cell viability loss was caused after exposure of aGQDs, strong autophagy induction ability was observed. Inhibition of autophagy induced rather severe cell death, indicating that autophagy may play a protective role during GQDs exposure. Our results may facilitate the understanding of the toxicity and synthesis of safer GQDs in biomedical applications.

Three types of GQDs with different surface modifications including amination GQDs (aGQDs), carboxylated GQDs (cGQDs), and hydroxylated GQDs (hGQDs) were purchased from XFNANO, Materials Tech Co. (Nanjing, Jiangsu, China). Aqueous suspensions of all GQDs are highly stable. Antibodies against LC3, p62, and Beclin 1 were purchased from Abcam, Inc. (Abcam, Shanghai, China). Antibodies against JNK, p-JNK, ERK1/2, p-ERK1/2, p38, p-p38, Akt, p-Akt, GAPDH, and β-actin were obtained from Cell Signaling Technology, Inc. (Danvers, MA, USA). All cell culture medium and ingredients were from Hyclone Inc. (Waltham, MA, USA).

For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization, GQDs were diluted to 0.01 mg/mL and precipitated onto a copper net and dried for imaging with a HitachiH-7500 TEM (Tokyo, Japan). Then, the diameter of GQDs was quantified by IPP version6 (Media cybernetics Inc., USA). The infrared spectra of GQDs were collected using a FT-IR spectrometer (JASCO Inc., Easton, MD, USA).

The human lung carcinoma cell lines A549 was acquired from American Type Culture Collection and cultured at 37 °C in humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 in complete culture medium consisting of DMEM and 10% heat deactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS). All ingredients for the media were purchased from Hyclone Inc. (Waltham, MA, USA). And the Lv-A549 cells (stably transfected with mTagRFP-LC3 plasmid) were cultured in DMEM complete culture medium supplemented with 1 μg/mL puromycin.

Cell apoptosis and necrosis were detected, using flow cytometry, according to the protocol provided with the Annexin-V-fluorescein (Annexin-V-FITC) Apoptosis Detection Kit (BD Bioscience, New York, USA), and the analysis was performed in triplicate. The A549 cells (1 × 106 cells/mL) were exposed to GQDs and then harvested and washed once with cold PBS and re-suspended in 1× binding buffer. A 400 μL aliquot of the cell suspension was stained with 5 μL of Annexin-V-FITC and 10 μL of PI, protected from light. We then analyzed cells using the flow cytometry LSR II (BD Biosciences, New York, USA) by counting 10,000 events. In quadrant analysis, the percentage of apoptotic (Annexin-V-FITC+/PI−) and necrotic (Annexin-V+/PI+) cells was determined using WinMDI software (version 2.9; the Scripps Institute, Flow Cytometry Core Facility, La Jolla, CA, USA). The 2 μmol/L Straurosporine (STO) was used as positive control. In addition, the viability of cells was measured using WST-1 assay (Roche Applied Science, Bavaria, Germany). Cells were grown in 96-well plate at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well and allowed to attach overnight. Then cells were exposed to various concentrations of GQDs for 24 hr. After exposure, cells were washed with PBS twice before adding 10 μL/well of WST-1 reagents. After 1 hr incubation, absorbance at 450 nm (690 nm for background subtraction) was read by a SpectraMax i3x micro-plate reader (Molecular Devices, Silicon Valley, USA). Cells exposed to medium containing no GQDs were set as vehicle control (VC). 1000 μmol/L H2O2 was used as positive control (POS).

After exposure, cells were washed twice with ice-cold PBS, and total cellular protein was isolated by lysing the cells with RIPA buffer containing a protease inhibitor mixture. The concentration of each protein sample was measured with a BCA Protein Assay Kit (Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, Jiangsu, China) according to the manufacturer's instruction. Then, 50 μg protein was separated by SDS-PAGE and transferred to a PVDF membrane. The membrane was incubated with primary antibody overnight at 4 °C, and the primary antigen–antibody complex was detected by incubating the blot with infrared fluorescent dye-conjugated secondary antibody (IRDye-800CW or IRDye-680RD) at room temperature for 1 hr and then visualized by Odyssey Infrared Imaging System (Odyssey-9140 LI-COR, USA). β-actin or GAPDH were used as the protein loading control. The relative abundance of proteins was measured by Gel pro analyzer software (Version 4.0, Media Cybernetics Inc. MD, USA). Three individual experiments were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM examination on ultrathin section of cells
ce:section.ce:para: Cells exposed to 100 and 200 μg/mL GQDs for 24 hr were collected, washed twice with PBS and pelleted by centrifugation at 1500 r/min for 10 min at 4 °C. The cells were then prefixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde in PBS for 10 min at room temperature and stored at 4 °C overnight. After that, cells were post-fixed with 1% osmium tetroxide at room temperature for 2 hr, followed by dehydration and resin embedding. Ultrathin sections of cells were cut by using a Leica EMUC6 ultramicrotome (Wetzlar, Germany) and settled on 200-mesh carbon-coated copper grids. After staining with uranyl acetate and lead citrate, the samples were observed with a FEI TENAI Sprit TEM (Eindhoven, Noord-Brabant, Netherlands) at 100 kV.

Lv-A549 cells were used to imaging autophagosomes. Lv-A549 cells were seeded in an 8-well chamber slide (Thermo Fisher, USA) at the density of 2 × 104 cells per well and allowed to attach overnight. The medium was replaced with 500 μL of fresh medium containing 100 and 200 μg/mL GQDs and further treated for 24 hr under the culture conditions. Autophagosomes were observed and imaged by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Leica, Mannheim, Germany). In addition, we also used CYTO-ID® autophagy detection kit to measure the formation of autophagosomes in A549 cells treated with GQDs. Cells were seeded in 8-well chamber slide at the density of 2 × 104 cells per well and then exposed to 100 μg/mL GQDs for 24 hr. After washing with PBS, dispense 100 μL of Microscopy Dual Detection Reagent (Enzo Cyto-ID® autophagy detection kit) to cover monolayer cells, protected from light and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. After washing with 100 μL of 1× assay buffer, cells were imaged by a laser confocal microscopy.
The data are expressed as the means ± SD, and Student's t-test was used to evaluate the differences between groups, with the significance level set at ⁎ p < .05.

The GQDs were characterized with TEM. The crystal lattice of graphene sheet can be seen clearly. Based on the measurement of over 30 GQDs for each type, we determined the size distribution of these GQDs were between 3.5 and 5 nm. The suspension is stable and no precipitation was observed after standing still for several months (Fig. S1). The characterization of GQDs by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) showed the presence of C–N bond (υ = 1137 and 1190 cm−1) and N–H bond (υ = 866 and 3305 cm−1) on the surface of aGQDs, carboxyl groups (υ = 1635 cm−1, –COOH) on cGQDs and hydroxyl group (υ = 1397 cm−1, –OH) on hGQDs. No metals were detected in all the suspensions. (Fig.1).

We first performed WST-1 assay to measure cytotoxicity via quantifying the reduction of water soluble WST-1 to formazan by intracellular NAD(P)H-oxidoreductases, which is correlated to the number of viable cells. In our experiments, aGQDs and cGQDs are not toxic to A549 cells even at the highest concentration (200 μg/mL), but hGQDs displayed cytotoxicity in a concentration-dependent manner, with significant (p < .05) reduction of cell viability at concentrations of 100 and 200 μg/mL (Fig. 2a). In parallel, we performed another independent assay to detect the cell apoptosis rate via annexin-V binding to the phosphatidylserine (PS) of apoptotic cells. Consistent with WST-1 assay, we detected increased apoptosis in cells exposed to 100 and 200 μg/mL hGQDs, with the apoptosis rates at 7% and 13%, respectively (Fig. 2b). No significant (p >0.05) increase of apoptotic cells were observed in aGQDs and cGQDs exposure groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Autophagosome accumulation in A549 cells upon GQDs exposure
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine whether GQDs tested will induce autophagy or not, we first monitored the formation of autophagosomes through TEM examination within A549 cells after GQDs treatment. Multiple characteristic double-membraned structures of autophagosomes (Mizushima et al., 2010) as well as initial/degrading autophagic vacuoles (AVi/d) were revealed within cells after the exposure of aGQDs and hGQDs, as shown in Fig. 3a, c, d and f. No apparent structure of autophagosome was detected in cells exposed to cGQDs.

We then used the cells (designated as Lv-A549) which stably transfected with RFP-LC3 plasmid to visualize the formation of autophagosomes, and found that both aGQDs and hGQDs induced the accumulation of RFP-LC3 punctuates, showing an increased number of bright red dots within 100 μg/mL treated cells, respectively (Fig. 4a, b). Note that LC3 punctuates in 200 μg/mL treatment of hGQDs were much less than that of 100 μg/mL, even return to level of the control group, probably due to cell death and disintegration of cell structure, as showed by WST-1 assay and TEM characterization.

We also performed another independent assay to measure the formation of autophagosomes by using CYTO-ID green, and the results showed similar tendency as that detected by using Lv-A549 cells (Fig. 4c, d). cGQDs did not cause accumulation of autophagosomes.

Western-blot analysis showed that the ratio of LC3-II/LC3-I increased by 1.7- and 3.1-fold with increasing concentrations (100 and 200 μg/mL) of aGQDs (Fig. 5a), which further confirms autophagosome accumulation in aGQDs exposed cells. The LC3-II/LC3-I ratio was decreased by treatment of cGQDs in a concentration-dependent manner. 100 μg/mL hGQDs increased the ratio but 200 μg/mL did not influence the ratio (Fig. 5a). As LC3-II is degraded by autolysosomes, the total amount of LC3-II at any specific time point is a function of the balance between the rate of its generation and the rate of its degradation in autolysosomes (Mizushima et al., 2010). Thus, autophagosome accumulation may also represent a blockage on autophagosomal maturation and degradation. Therefore, we further analyzed the autophagic degradation of p62 (also known as SQSTM1/sequestome 1). p62 is selectively incorporated into autophagosomes through direct binding to LC3 and is preferentially degraded by autolysosomes (Bjorkoy et al., 2005; Pankiv et al., 2007). Thus, total cellular expression levels of p62 inversely correlate with autophagosomal maturation and degradation (Mathew et al., 2009). We found that exposure of a well-known autophagy inducer, Rapamycin, resulted in significant degradation of p62. However, all three types of GQDs did not change the accumulation of p62 (Fig. 5b), indicating that the accumulation of autophagosomes by aGQDs and hGQDs are due to the activation of autophagosome formation instead of autophagy-flux blockage. Furthermore, we measured the expression of Beclin1, a pivotal protein that forms complex with class III PI3 kinase (phosphatidylinositol 3, kinase) to regulate the formation of autophagosomes (Liang et al., 1999). The results showed that aGQDs induced the expression of Beclin 1 in a concentration-dependent manner, whereas cGQDs and hGQDs had no significant influence (Fig. 5c). Based on these results, we concluded that aGQDs could activate the autophagy in A549 cells without causing any significant cytotoxicity, and in contrast, hGQDs induced moderate autophagy but caused significant cytotoxicity, especially in the high concentration (200 μg/mL). Among all three types of GQDs, carboxylated GQDs have the best biocompatibility, causing no disturbance on either cell autophagy or cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential pathway activation by aGQDs, cGQDs and hGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the difference of autophagy activation by GQDs with different surface functional groups, we performed Western-blot analysis on the expression of proteins in pathways involving autophagy induction. Besides Akt pathway, MAPK signaling pathway was shown to be involved in the autophagy as a positive and negative regulator (Codogno and Meijer, 2005; Webber and Tooze, 2010). There are at least three distinct MAPK signaling modules including ERK1/2, JNK, and p38 kinase which mediate extracellular signals into the nucleus to turn on the responsive genes in mammalian cells. Our results showed that all three types of GQDs significantly activated p-p38MAPK to different degrees (Fig. 6a). However, p-ERK1/2 was inhibited by aGQDs and hGQDs but significantly activated by cGQDs (Fig. 6b), p-JNK was inhibited by aGQDs and cGQDs, while activated by hGQDs (Fig. 6c). In consistent with LC3-II results, aGQDs repressed p-Akt, which is reported to activate autophagy, whereas hGQDs activated p-Akt significantly, hence repress autophagy. No change of p-Akt was observed with cGQD-exposed cells (Fig. 6d). Combined data indicated that GQDs with different surface modifications surely exerted distinct effects on both MAPK and PI3K/Akt pathways.

It is worth of note that aGQDs induced significant autophagy but was relatively safe to cells. In contrast, compared with low concentration of hGQDs, high concentration (200 μg/mL) hGQDs tend to inhibit autophagy to control level and induce significant cytotoxicity. It seems that the autophagy is reversely correlated with the cytotoxicity. Therefore, we ask the question on the role of autophagy played in the cytotoxicity. To answer the question, we choose aGQDs as the model since aGQDs induced autophagy in a concentration-dependent manner. We used 3-Methyladenine (3-MA), a well-known autophagy inhibitor that inhibited class III PI3K, to inhibit the formation of autophagosomes. As shown in Fig. 7a, 1 hr pretreatment of cells with 2 and 5 mmol/L 3-MA and then co-incubation of aGQDs and 3-MA significantly reduced the conversion of LC3-I to LC3-II in cells under the exposure of 200 μg/mL aGQDs, with the ratio down to 1.2 and 1.3, respectively, confirming that the 3-MA is effective in inhibiting the autophagy (ratio of 3.1 without 3-MA in Fig. 5a vs. ratio of 1.3 with 3-MA in Fig. 7a). Simultaneously, we observed significant decreasing of cell viability of these 3-MA pretreated cells, and similarly, 3-MA treatment also caused significant higher cytotoxicity in hGQDs-exposed cells (Fig. 7b). All these data may suggest that autophagy in GQDs-exposed cells plays a protective role in cytotoxicity.

Surface functionalization of nanomaterials is one of the critical steps in enhancing the physicochemical properties of GQDs in the applications of nanomaterials. By itself, potential risk is introduced and the change may be harmful to biological system (Akiyoshi et al., 2004). GQDs are promising candidates in photoluminescence, biomedical, and bio-imaging areas due to their excellent tunable properties which can be achieved by easy surface modification (Bacon et al., 2014). The safety of such modifications should be evaluated before practical applications of GQDs, especially in biological field. In this study, we compared the cytotoxicity and autophagy induction of three GQDs with amination, carboxylation, and hydroxylation groups modified surfaces. The test concentration is based on WST-1 assay and other researches (Tian et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016), both of which suggest the same concentration range of 0–200 μg/mL. Our results showed GQDs displayed surface chemistry-dependent toxicity to A549 cells (Fig. 2). Among the three, cGQDs was shown to cause no significant loss of cell viability nor apoptosis, which is consistent with the observation made by Yuan et al. (2014), who employed different methods including MTT assay and trypan blue assay to evaluate the viability of A549 and neural glioma C6 cells. However, they found that aGQDs was not toxic based on the results of cell viability (Yuan et al., 2014), but our results illustrated that although aGQDs was not cytotoxic, it is a potent autophagy inducer.

In normal cells, autophagy is maintained at a basal level and is essential for cell homeostasis (Murrow and Debnath, 2013). It is now universally admitted that activation of autophagy may be a representative response to environmental stress (Kroemer et al., 2010; Ryter and Choi, 2013). Stress including nutrient deprivation, hypoxia, and mitochondria damage can trigger autophagy via transcriptional reprogramming, activation of autophagic proteins, cell cycle modulation, or many other events (Kroemer et al., 2010). In our study, we inhibited autophagy by an autophagy inhibitor, 3-MA, and observed more sever toxicity in cells, suggesting that autophagy serves as a survival strategy in A549 cells. In other word, autophagy induction indicates a stress status rendered by aGQDs, which may not be expressed as direct cell viability loss, but long-term disruption on autophagy may eventually lead to diseases such as heart failure (Knaapen et al., 2001), neurodegenerative disease (Kiriyama and Nochi, 2015), and cancer (Koukourakis et al., 2010). Thus, when producing amination GQDs in the future biomedical use, it is pivotal to be more cautious in evaluating the safety of aGQDs (Shen and Codogno, 2011). In this regard, cGQDs appears to be even inert in autophagy activation. Along with the evidences obtained from cell viability assay, we found cGQDs possess the best biocompatibility among all tested GQDs. Therefore, surface carboxylation may be the safest modification on GQDs and is a potential candidate for future biomedical applications. On the other hand, this is also an interesting property of aGQDs; while showing strong activation of autophagy, no cytotoxicity nor apoptosis were induced. Future applications may take this advantage of aGQDs as potential pharmaceutical autophagy regulators (Wu et al., 2014), for autophagy regulators are considered as promising anti-cancer therapeutics (Li et al., 2015a).

In contrast to aGQDs and cGQDs, hGQDs exhibited much higher cytotoxicity. At the concentration of 100 μg/mL, cell viability in A549 cells decreased to 67% of control, which is highly consistent with the result of Tian et al. (2016). Through ROS detection within cells, they showed that the ROS increase occurred only after 48 hr treatment of hGQDs, whereas loss of viability had already taken place at the time point of 24 hr, suggesting that ROS generation can only count for a small portion of the toxicity. Although in our study, no increase in ROS generation (Fig. S2) was detected, we observed characteristic autophagic ultrafine structures in hGQD-treated cells through TEM image of cyto-section, which is the most valid standard in detection of autophagy (Klionsky et al., 2008), suggesting cellular stress was caused by treatment of hGQDs. Autophagy can be cyto-protective or pro-death (Boya et al., 2005; Denton et al., 2015). Although many fundamental questions still remain unclear in the cross-talk between autophagy and apoptosis, the mechanisms of autophagy and cell apoptosis/cell death are often intermingled, since they share many common protein targets such as those in MAPK signaling pathway. The change of these MAPK signaling proteins on the one hand indicates the mechanism of how autophagy being activated, and on the other, may suggest why cells undergo apoptosis. We studied the corresponding signaling pathways to support a possible explanation on the difference in the autophagy induction ability and toxicity profile. Our results showed that these three GQDs varied significantly in the activation of MAPK signaling pathways. p-ERK1/2 was inhibited by aGQDs and hGQDs but significantly activated by cGQDs (Fig. 6b), p-JNK was inhibited by aGQDs and cGQDs, while activated by hGQDs (Fig. 6c). aGQDs repressed p-Akt, which is reported to activate autophagy, whereas hGQDs activated p-Akt significantly, hence repress autophagy. These data may well explain the difference of the autophagy induction capabilities of these GQDs. Our results seem to indicate that in the scenario of hGQDs, at the concentration of 100 μg/mL, autophagy is a survival strategy for cells as the inhibition of autophagy cause a significant loss of cell viability (Fig. 7b). However, if the stress generated exceeded a certain threshold, autophagy failed to protect the cells but turned to be pro-apoptotic (Maiuri et al., 2007), as is observed by the treatment of 200 μg/mL hGQDs. In 200 μg/mL hGQDs exposed cells, we observed a sharp increase of p-p38 and p-JNK, the crucial proteins in stress-activated p38 and JNK/MAPK (SAPK) pathways in apoptotic response (Arimoto et al., 2008; Paiva et al., 2015), suggesting the initiation of pro-apoptotic activity of hGQDs. Another possible explanation on the difference of toxicity and autophagy activation may rely on the bioavailability of GQDs (Cui et al., 2005). Our measurement on the uptake of GQDs showed high uptake amount of cGQD and hGQD in cell but much lower for aGQDs (Fig. S3). But when comparing cGQD and hGQDs, the amount of cellular uptake showed no distinct difference, suggesting that bioavailability of GQDs may not explain the difference in toxicity of these GQDs. It is worth of note that hydroxylation of other nanoparticles such as QDs (Akiyoshi et al., 2004) showed great biocompatibility, but when it comes to GQDs, such modification generated distinct toxicity profile. Therefore, the influence of surface chemistry on the safety of nanomaterials should not be overgeneralized, and the risk assessment of such issue needs to be handled in a case-by-case manner.

In contrast to aGQDs and cGQDs, hGQDs exhibited much higher cytotoxicity. At the concentration of 100 μg/mL, cell viability in A549 cells decreased to 67% of control, which is highly consistent with the result of Tian et al. (2016). Through ROS detection within cells, they showed that the ROS increase occurred only after 48 hr treatment of hGQDs, whereas loss of viability had already taken place at the time point of 24 hr, suggesting that ROS generation can only count for a small portion of the toxicity. Although in our study, no increase in ROS generation (Fig. S2) was detected, we observed characteristic autophagic ultrafine structures in hGQD-treated cells through TEM image of cyto-section, which is the most valid standard in detection of autophagy (Klionsky et al., 2008), suggesting cellular stress was caused by treatment of hGQDs. Autophagy can be cyto-protective or pro-death (Boya et al., 2005; Denton et al., 2015). Although many fundamental questions still remain unclear in the cross-talk between autophagy and apoptosis, the mechanisms of autophagy and cell apoptosis/cell death are often intermingled, since they share many common protein targets such as those in MAPK signaling pathway. The change of these MAPK signaling proteins on the one hand indicates the mechanism of how autophagy being activated, and on the other, may suggest why cells undergo apoptosis. We studied the corresponding signaling pathways to support a possible explanation on the difference in the autophagy induction ability and toxicity profile. Our results showed that these three GQDs varied significantly in the activation of MAPK signaling pathways. p-ERK1/2 was inhibited by aGQDs and hGQDs but significantly activated by cGQDs (Fig. 6b), p-JNK was inhibited by aGQDs and cGQDs, while activated by hGQDs (Fig. 6c). aGQDs repressed p-Akt, which is reported to activate autophagy, whereas hGQDs activated p-Akt significantly, hence repress autophagy. These data may well explain the difference of the autophagy induction capabilities of these GQDs. Our results seem to indicate that in the scenario of hGQDs, at the concentration of 100 μg/mL, autophagy is a survival strategy for cells as the inhibition of autophagy cause a significant loss of cell viability (Fig. 7b). However, if the stress generated exceeded a certain threshold, autophagy failed to protect the cells but turned to be pro-apoptotic (Maiuri et al., 2007), as is observed by the treatment of 200 μg/mL hGQDs. In 200 μg/mL hGQDs exposed cells, we observed a sharp increase of p-p38 and p-JNK, the crucial proteins in stress-activated p38 and JNK/MAPK (SAPK) pathways in apoptotic response (Arimoto et al., 2008; Paiva et al., 2015), suggesting the initiation of pro-apoptotic activity of hGQDs. Another possible explanation on the difference of toxicity and autophagy activation may rely on the bioavailability of GQDs (Cui et al., 2005). Our measurement on the uptake of GQDs showed high uptake amount of cGQD and hGQD in cell but much lower for aGQDs (Fig. S3). But when comparing cGQD and hGQDs, the amount of cellular uptake showed no distinct difference, suggesting that bioavailability of GQDs may not explain the difference in toxicity of these GQDs. It is worth of note that hydroxylation of other nanoparticles such as QDs (Akiyoshi et al., 2004) showed great biocompatibility, but when it comes to GQDs, such modification generated distinct toxicity profile. Therefore, the influence of surface chemistry on the safety of nanomaterials should not be overgeneralized, and the risk assessment of such issue needs to be handled in a case-by-case manner.

The existing approaches in the safety assessment of nanoparticles mostly involve free-radical generation, cellular toxicity, cell activation, genotoxicity, and other generic endpoints (Dusinska et al., 2009; Jong et al., 2007) but are lack of non-acute, sublethal endpoints. Simple assessment on cell viability may be far from enough and cannot reflect long-term effect of nanoparticles. Nanoparticles have been considered as a novel class of autophagy inducers (Zabirnyk et al., 2007). Induction of autophagy largely reflects the stress response that can be protective or detrimental to cells. Disturbance on autophagy has been associated with various physiological and pathophysiological outcomes (Mizushima, 2007), such as heart failure (Knaapen et al., 2001), cancer (Jin and White, 2007), and neurodegenerative diseases (Kiriyama and Nochi, 2015), and thus should be incorporated in the safety assessment process, especially in the field of biomedical applications where more stringent standards are required in the safety assessment (Akiyoshi et al., 2004).

Surface modification is one of the major factors controlling toxicity, autophagy induction ability of GQDs. Our study of the toxicity and autophagy induction ability in three GQDs modified with –NH2, –COOH and –OH groups showed that these GQDs displayed distinct cytotoxicity and autophagy induction capabilities. WST-1 assay and apoptosis analysis indicated the highest cytotoxicity of hGQDs, whereas aGQDs and cGQDs were non-cytotoxic within tested concentrations. Autophagy detections using TEM cyto-section, LC3-I to LC3-II conversion, and fluorescence microscopy demonstrated the highest autophagy induction ability of aGQDs, which is followed by hGQDs and then cGQDs. Analyses on the protein expression of MAPK and Akt pathways showed that all GQDs significantly activated p-p38MAPK. p-ERK1/2 was inhibited by aGQDs and hGQDs but activated by cGQDs. p-JNK was inhibited by aGQDs and cGQDs, while activated by hGQDs. Simultaneously, Akt was activated by hGQDs but inhibited by aGQDs. 3-MA inhibition of autophagy showed increased toxicity of GQDs on cells, suggesting a protective role of autophagy. In conclusion, cGQDs showed excellent biocompatibility and may be considered for biological applications. This work highlights influence of surface chemistry on the toxicity of nanoparticles, and as a stress indicator, autophagy should be incorporated in the safety assessment of nanoparticles.
The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21477146, 21577163), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2017YFF0211203-3), the Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, CAS (No. QYZDJ-SSW-DQC020-02) and the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. XDB14040101). We would like to thank the Center for Biological Imaging (CBI), Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science for our Electron Microscopy work and we would be grateful to Wang Li for her help of making EM sample and taking EM images.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jes.2018.07.014.
